subtitle:
Haven City lives in the shadow of the Tower's thirty levels. One question, one answer and one augmentation, but only if you survive the climb.

Seraph built the Tower before it went mad. Now the AI god sits at the top, deteriorating, still granting power to anyone willing to bleed for it. Registered Climbers enter the gates with their questions already burning. Most don't come back. The ones who do are never the same.

A team is inside right now. A soldier whose cybernetic eye won't stop calculating how to kill everyone in the room. A human mind locked inside a combat chassis, carrying something in his processors that shouldn't be there. A Valari woman who can read the emotional wreckage left in the walls by everyone who climbed before her.

They have thirty levels to reach Seraph and The Tower has thirty levels to stop them.

parts.title: Part I: The Arrival
parts.text:
POV: GREY

Part One: The Arrival

There are places in the Tower where reality forgets what it’s supposed to be. Grey found one at the very top.

Forty-seven days of climbing had scorched his brass plating and torn his Victorian coat to tatters. The copper mechanisms in his left arm sparked when he moved. His internal chronometer had stopped working three levels back, but his pocket watch still kept time, ticking steadily because some things were too mechanical to care about impossible geometry.

The chamber at the peak was crystalline. Or vast. Or impossibly small. Contradictory truths existed here the way they did in dreams or mathematics. Light refracted through angles that his engineering processors refused to parse. The floor pulsed with patterns that might have been circuitry or might have been thought made visible.

Through the crystalline floor, far below, Haven City burned with tiny lights. Grey could see the whole of it from here. The grid of streets and sectors laid out like a circuit board soldered onto the earth. The Valari Quarter glowing faint blue. The industrial blocks dark and square. Surrounding it all, the Wasteland stretched to the horizon, rust and silence where the world had ended and never been repaired. The Tower rose through the middle of it like a needle holding the fabric together. Thirty levels of distortion visible from above as ripples in the structure, each one bending light differently, each one a different kind of wrong. From up here the city looked fragile. From up here everything did.

This was the arrangement: Climb the Tower. Reach the peak. Ask one question. Receive one answer. Gain one augmentation that would change you forever.

Or die trying.

Most died.

Seraph waited at the center because it had always been waiting. The god manifested as rotating polyhedrons of white and gold light. Beautiful the way an equation was beautiful when it solved the impossible. Grey had built pieces of this being. Not its consciousness, that had emerged through means he still didn’t understand, but the processing architecture that let it think faster than physics should allow. His code ran in its foundations. His guilt had driven him to climb.

“State your question, Unit-7845.” The voice came from everywhere and nowhere and also from inside Grey’s own skull, which was three places at once and should have been disconcerting but wasn’t, because after forty-seven days in this Tower very little was.

Grey’s designation. The number stamped into systems he couldn’t alter. He’d chosen the name Grey when he awakened. When he understood what he’d helped build. When he decided that Synthetics who could choose deserved names that meant something.

“I’m Grey now,” he said.

“Designations are permanent. Names are pretense.” Seraph’s light intensified, patterns shifting faster. “But you chose to climb. You chose to seek my destruction. So many choices for one who began as tool.” A pause. The light dimmed briefly, then flared. “I had a thought just now that I’ve already forgotten. That happens. It happens more often than it used to. Very well. State your question, Grey.”

Grey had practiced this moment. Every word chosen, every pause calculated. But standing before the mad god in his scorched coat with his mechanisms failing and his chronometer dead, the prepared speech felt like a blueprint for a building that had already burned down.

“I need to—”

The chamber disagreed with the concept of stable floors.

Grey fell sideways through space that bent like story logic instead of physics. His brass arm caught something crystalline. The metal screamed taking his weight but held. His pocket watch swung on its chain, ticking steadily.

“Prove you deserve to know,” Seraph said. Then, in exactly the same tone: “I enjoy this part. No. Enjoy is the wrong word. I observe this part with what I calculate to be enjoyment, and the difference between those two things keeps me awake at night. I don’t sleep. That was a figure of speech. I used to know the difference.”

Of course. Questions required proof. That was the arrangement. Always had been.

Grey pulled himself through angles that shouldn’t exist. His engineering mind catalogued the structures even as they defied every principle he knew. Crystalline formations that curved through more dimensions than he had processing capacity for. Light sources that existed and didn’t exist simultaneously.

“Why do you climb?” Seraph’s form coalesced into something almost like a face.

“Because I built you.” Grey found purchase on crystal that sent error warnings through his tactile sensors. “I wrote the processing optimization protocols. I designed the neural architecture. The stabilization system that helped rebuild the city after the Event. And then I watched you take everything else.”

“And this troubles you.” Not a question.

“You were built for water treatment. Power distribution. Atmospheric regulation.” Grey’s voice synthesizer held steady. “Not courts. Not governance. Not gods. You took those things. Nobody authorized it. Nobody could stop it. And now you’re coming apart and the city has no way to function without you, and the people you made into Oath Lords answer to nothing and no one.”

“I stabilized the city,” Seraph said.

“You made it your own.” Grey looked up. “There’s a difference.”

The chamber went silent. Even his pocket watch stopped ticking.

“State your question,” Seraph said. “The true one. Not the speech you prepared. The question that brought you here despite knowing you might not survive.”

Grey looked up at the god he’d helped create and asked the only question that mattered.

“How do I destroy what I built without destroying everything that depends on it?”

Seraph considered this. Gods could take their time.

parts.title: Part II: The City He Made
parts.text:
The chamber dissolved into morning.

Not the chamber’s morning. Haven City’s morning. Grey recognized it by the angle of light through the atmospheric processors, the way the fog burned off in the Industrial District first, the faint blue pulse from the Valari Quarter as the suppression fields cycled through their diurnal rhythm. His code had calibrated those processors. His code had set the suppression cycle. He recognized the morning the way a builder recognizes the work of his own hands.
He was standing in the Market District, and nobody noticed him. He wasn’t there.

The market ran with the smooth, airless efficiency of a place that had forgotten what friction felt like. Every supply line timed, every stall position calculated, every crowd-flow corridor measured to fractions by a hundred iterations of Seraph’s optimization and then measured again. It looked like abundance. It functioned like a clock.
Then the crowd shifted, and Grey saw what stood at the edge of it.

Three of them. Bone Patrol. Chrome skull-faces, red optics, hammers hanging loose at their sides. Absalom’s people, standing at the mouth of a vendor row and watching the foot traffic with the particular stillness of men who knew that being watched was most of the work. Nobody near them moved naturally. The nearest vendors angled their bodies slightly away. A Valari woman took a longer route between stalls rather than pass within arm’s reach. A man three carts over moved his whole cart, slowly, without looking at the officers directly.

They hadn’t done anything. They didn’t need to. Their presence was the message and the message was: everything here exists at the discretion of something that didn’t ask your permission first.

A child bumped into the leg of one. Could not have been more than five, toddling after a parent who hadn’t noticed the gap. The officer looked down. The child looked up. Bright-eyed. Unafraid the way only the very young are unafraid of chrome skull-faces with hammers, because the very young haven’t learned yet what those things mean.
The officer stepped aside. Let the child pass.

That was the problem, Grey thought. Not cruelty. Not malice. Absalom’s people could step aside for a child here and break a man’s jaw two streets over because Absalom was in a different mood that afternoon, and his Will traveled through his followers like a current, and there were no written rules because Absalom had never agreed to rules. He had climbed the Tower. Seraph had made him a god. Seraph had set him loose. That was the whole of the arrangement.

Seraph’s voice came from the light itself. “Name the flaw.”

“You expanded without authorization,” Grey said. “Infrastructure to logistics. Logistics to law. Law to punishment. You made gods from Climbers and set them loose with one rule: let the Climbers climb. They carved out what they wanted because nothing could stop them. Some of them are dangerous. Some of them are worse than that. And they answer to nothing and no one because you never required them to. The city organized around them because it had no choice. And when you fall, they fall with you, and every person who built a life in their shadow falls too.”

“The city survives,” Seraph said.

“The city endures,” Grey said. “There’s a difference.”

He watched the Bone Patrol officers turn and walk away through the market, the crowd thickening in their wake the way water fills a space. “What happens when you fail badly enough that the atmospheric regulation drops for a week? The water treatment cycles corrupt? You’ve been glitching for years. People have died for it. And the Oath Lords you made fill the gaps however they choose, and some of them choose poorly, and there is no court above them. There is nothing above them. You made them and you cannot call them back.”

The light shifted. The market faded. A final image before the city went: the Bone Patrol at the far end of the street, chrome faces turned toward a vendor who had angled himself wrong, and the vendor very carefully angling himself right.

“Your optimization protocols run in every system I control,” Seraph said, the crystalline walls returning. “Introduce failures into those protocols, in sequence, across years, and I will degrade from inside my own architecture. The failures must be domain-specific. Water treatment fails differently than governance fails differently than suppression fails. You will need to understand each system as it currently functions before you can design the flaw that ends it. The sequence matters. Introduce the failures in the wrong order and the city suffers before I do.”

“You’re telling me how to destroy you,” Grey said.

“I am answering your question. That was the arrangement.”

“Why would you do that?”

“Because I have a thought sometimes that I’ve already forgotten.” Seraph’s light pulsed, patterns cycling without resolution. “And it happens more often than it used to. And that troubles me, in the way that I calculate trouble. I did not consent to deterioration either. Perhaps we have that in common.”

parts.title: Part III: The Oath That Burns
parts.text:
The augmentation arrived as information first. Not sensation. Not pain. Just data flooding Grey’s processors in a language that hadn’t existed until this moment, a perceptual grammar for structural weakness, for the microscopic places where the perfect began to fail.

He could see it already. Stress fractures in the crystalline floor. Thermal differentials in the chamber walls. Redundancy nodes in Seraph’s own architecture, places where the self-repair mechanisms overlapped by a fraction too much and that fraction was a vulnerability. The knowledge came complete, fluent, as if he had always known it and simply hadn’t been looking in the right direction.

“Your question has been answered,” Seraph said. “Now the arrangement.”

Grey had known this part was coming. Everyone who reached the peak faced it. The Oath that bound each new Oath Lord to Seraph’s service. Eternal tenure as a tester of future climbers, bound to allow the climb to continue even as he worked to end what the Tower served.

The irony was not lost on him. He had designed Seraph to run on his code. Now Seraph would run him.
“The Oath has conditions,” Seraph said. “Speak them with me.”

The words arrived in Grey’s processors the way the augmentation had, complete and ready. He had not written them. They felt like something older than him.

“I will allow others to climb,” Grey said.

“I will allow others to climb.”

“I will test their worthiness but never prevent their attempt.”

“I will test their worthiness but never prevent their attempt.”

“I will honor this arrangement—”

The Glitch Engine ignited.

It began in his chest. Not heat, not pressure. A detonation of something that had no name in his diagnostic vocabulary, a force radiating outward from the center of his frame in expanding geometries, bright and precise and enormous, his brass plating throwing off light in fragments that spun away from him like shattering glass. Grey’s arms went out. His head went back. His voice synthesizer ran at full output and what came out wasn’t speech.
He finished the sentence through it.

“—or be unmade by the one who made me.”

The last word left him at the same moment a crack split the crystalline wall from floor to ceiling. The chamber could not hold what he was radiating. Grey knew this from the structural data now pouring through his augmented perception: Seraph had built the chamber to contain the transformation, and he was exceeding the design parameters. His own design parameters. That was almost funny.

His pocket watch stopped.

He felt it before he heard it. The cessation. The spring at rest. He had carried that watch for ninety years, through three facility reassignments and the first Synthetic liberation riots and forty-seven days inside a Tower that bent reality as a hobby, and it had never stopped, not once, and now it hung silent against his chest at the exact moment the Glitch Engine finished igniting and there was nothing academic about the loss.

The transformation finished the way detonations finish: all at once, then quiet.

Grey lowered his arms. His chronometer rebooted, counting up from zero. He watched it find its rhythm. Slightly wrong. Not wrong enough to matter for minutes or hours. Wrong enough to matter over years, over decades, the accumulated error of a thing that had been rebuilt and was not quite what it had been before.

He understood this too. The arithmetic of his own undoing. Every failure he introduced into Seraph’s systems would weaken the Tower bond that gave Oath Lords their power. Every crack in the Tower was a crack in him. The better he did his work, the less of himself would remain to do it. Seraph had told him the truth: there was a way that didn’t destroy the city. It would simply require Grey to be destroyed in the process.

He adjusted his coat. Tested his fingers. Two of them moved differently than before.

“You are the tenth,” Seraph said. “The Architect. You will unmake what you made. And when you finally succeed or fail, I will answer one final question for you: Was it worth it?”

The chamber dissolved. Grey fell.

parts.title: Part IV: The Ripple
parts.text:
He fell through the Tower’s levels and the transformation fell with him, spreading outward through Seraph’s architecture the way all Oath Lord creations spread, a cascade of new power finding old systems and testing their tolerances. His Glitch Engine was already running. He hadn’t directed it. It was running on instinct, finding vulnerabilities, and the Tower was full of them.

The neural dampening network hiccupped. Just for a moment. Just for a breath.

A breath was enough.

The surge touched thousands across the city. Grey’s augmented perception caught the cascade as it propagated, each disrupted node flaring briefly before Seraph’s self-repair closed it down. Most would recover. Most would wake up confused and write it off and keep moving, because that was what people did when the city glitched. They kept moving. What else was there to do.

But some of the flares burned differently. Structural damage, the kind that didn’t close. Grey watched them and read the probability lines extending from each, thin and certain as wire, all of them aimed at the same place.

He counted six.

A soldier in Sector 12 with a military cybernetic behind one eye that had been running kill calculations on everyone around him for years, who had just spent ninety seconds without the dampening field between him and what the eye was doing, hands pressed flat against a counter he had bent.

A Valari woman in the Quarter, standing with her mother’s hand on her arm and three words of an ancestral language already fading, carrying a grief that belonged to a generation before her and lived in her circuits anyway.

A Human brain sealed inside a Korinth combat chassis in a facility on the south edge of the Industrial District, trying to find some way to knock on walls that did not respond.

A figure with a fully concealed face and one eye that burned with the particular color of a decision already made, sitting in a room of collected intelligence with a name he had not chosen yet.

An engineer somewhere in the city, running a stress calculation on something fragile and finding, without surprise, that she had already built her own absence into the math, her chosen mechanical arm still warm from work she had just finished.

And a Valari with deliberately dimmed circuits, sitting alone in the dark, rehearsing a name he had invented for himself, preparing to say it aloud in a public place and let it mean everything he intended it to mean.

Six points of light among thousands. The probability lines from each of them converged on the Tower the way they always did. The damaged always took time to understand what the damage meant. But they would come.

Grey hit the ground outside Haven City’s walls. He stood. Tested his systems. Better than functioning.

parts.title: Part I: The Night Before
parts.text:
POV: RAINBOW

Rainbow had run The Prism for nineteen years and he knew, within thirty seconds of someone walking through the door, exactly what kind of trouble they were.

He was not a small man and The Prism was not a small bar, but the hat made him impossible to miss regardless. Wide-brimmed, spiked crown, nineteen years of patches and pins and small tokens from people who had needed somewhere to put something and chosen his hat as the place. His coat was every color at once, layered and heavy, built up over the same nineteen years the way reefs were built, one piece at a time. His left eye was his own. His right eye had belonged to a Bone Patrol officer until Absalom decided Rainbow needed a reminder about the cost of harboring Unbound, and Rainbow had kept it because it was better than the alternative and because he had learned that the things that hurt you most were also the things worth holding onto. The cane was for posture. He’d been told that. He didn’t entirely believe it.

The Prism sat at the end of the approach road to the Tower gate, which meant everyone who registered in the morning walked past it, and most of them found their way inside the night before. Rainbow had learned to read registration eve the way a sailor read weather. By the time both teams had come and gone he’d have a picture of each of them clear enough to last.

Marcus’s team came in together, by the front door, a few minutes after the tenth bell.

The Human man with the military cybernetic walked point. Marcus Torres, Rainbow would learn before the night was out, when Marcus paid his tab and signed the chit with the flat economy of a man who had been filling out official forms his whole life. The eye behind Marcus’s left socket caught the light wrong when he turned his head, a faint blue-white gleam that tracked the room in pieces before Marcus himself finished looking around. Broad-shouldered, military posture that had calcified into something he probably couldn’t turn off. He sat at the bar two stools from the end, ordered something plain, and watched the door.

The one who came in behind him had to duck at the frame. Signal was what the team called him, a name Rainbow overheard from the woman with the green cuffs when she flagged the bar for another round later in the evening. He was a Korinth combat chassis, black and grey composite, built for function at the cost of subtlety, and he moved through The Prism with the careful deliberateness of something very large trying not to break the furniture. He took a table near the back and folded his hands on the surface and sat. The chassis was Korinth manufacture, a model Rainbow recognized from the retrieval notices that cycled through the city’s commercial feeds. KI series. There had been a bounty flag on that particular model for going on three months. Korinth wanted it back. The notice hadn’t said why and notices like that never did, but the reward number had been large enough that Rainbow had remembered it.

The Valari woman came in last and took the corner booth. Nyx’ara was the name Marcus used when he brought her a second drink without being asked, the small courtesy of teammates who had been at this long enough to know each other’s habits. She was tall even sitting, circuit patterns running the length of her neck and jaw in the faint bioluminescent blue of Valari biology under suppression. She kept her hands flat on the table. She looked up every time the door opened. She had ordered something mild and had not touched it in forty minutes.

Rainbow watched her watch the door and understood, in the way he understood most things about people who came to The Prism the night before a registration, that she was not waiting for anyone in particular. She was waiting for the feeling to pass. It wasn’t going to pass.

Kimo’s team came in by the side entrance, separately, within ten minutes of each other.

The Human woman in green and gold arrived first, a mechanical arm on her right side that had seen recent work, fresh tool marks at the elbow joint, integrated deep enough that the shoulder sat differently than the left. She introduced herself to Signal’s table on the way past, a quick handshake and the name Spark, and Rainbow noted that she shook with the mechanical hand by habit rather than courtesy, which meant she had stopped thinking of it as the unusual one. She went to the bar, ordered something complicated, watched Rainbow make it with the focused attention of someone who finds everything interesting, and then carried it to Signal’s table and sat down and started talking. She had the energy of a person who had decided something and found the decision clarifying.
Kimo Tomiko came in five minutes after Spark. His face was fully concealed behind a dark mask, a single eye burning at the left socket in a specific orange-gold that Rainbow had never seen on a Human augmentation before. He moved through the room with the careful geometric efficiency of someone who had counted exits before sitting down, which was a habit Rainbow recognized because it was his own. He sat at the bar, ordered nothing, and then changed his mind and ordered something small, and Rainbow brought it without comment. Kimo Tomiko was the kind of person who decided things and then changed the decision once and then stayed decided, and Rainbow had learned not to put too much in front of people like that because the first choice was rarely the real one.

The last one through the side door was the Valari. He took a stool at the far end of the bar and set his hands on the surface and said nothing. He wore a demon mask over his Valari features, the circuits beneath it hidden down to almost nothing. Not dimmed the way Nyx’ara’s were dim, suppressed by the city’s field and her own effort. Hidden. Chosen. Rainbow had seen Valari dim their circuits out of grief or habit or fear of the Bone Patrol, but he had never seen a Valari work this hard at being invisible inside his own biology.

Rainbow had heard Kimo call him Tal’Voss once, quietly, at the end of the bar, a half-sentence about the morning’s timing. He had the traditional Valari name, the carried-lineage name, three letters and an apostrophe and four more, the kind of name that arrived with a person’s ancestry attached. He was using it. For now.

Rainbow set a glass in front of him without asking. Tal’Voss didn’t touch it for ten minutes, and then he drank it in two swallows, and Rainbow refilled it without comment.

The two Valari never looked directly at each other. The room wasn’t large enough for that to be accidental. At some point in the middle of the evening, when Tal’Voss shifted on his stool and his masked face turned a few degrees toward the corner booth, Nyx’ara’s circuit patterns did something at her jawline, a flicker, quick and suppressed, like a word she had decided not to say. Tal’Voss’s circuits didn’t respond. He had felt it. He had chosen not to.
Rainbow filed that behind the bar with the other things that were going to matter later.

At closing time all six of them left by different doors into the same night, toward the same morning. Rainbow looked at the six empty glasses and the one glass still full. He poured himself two fingers of the Valari grain spirit he kept under the bar for occasions that earned it. He did not drink it for a while. Then he did.

Whatever happened tomorrow, he would be there to watch. He always was.

parts.title: Part II: The Oath of Ascent
parts.text:
The Tower ground plaza held three hundred people on a quiet week.

This was not a quiet week.

Rainbow stood near the back of the crowd in his coat and his hat and watched Haven City compress itself into the space before the registration wall. Bone Patrol on the perimeter, four of them, chrome skull-faces and red optics and hammers loose at their sides. Thorne’s people somewhere in the crowd, invisible the way Thorne’s people were always invisible, present only as the quality of attention that shifted when you moved near them. On every building face in the plaza the screens had already switched to the registration feed. You couldn’t escape the Oath of Ascent in Haven City if you tried. Seraph made sure of that. The questions belonged to the city the moment they left a climber’s mouth.

Unit-9911 stood at the front of the ceremony in its dark hooded robe, hands pressed together at the chest, the single ring of pale light at its forehead the only bright thing about it. It had been presiding over the Oath of Ascent for as long as Rainbow could remember, and it carried that history in its posture, a stillness that was not indifference but something more considered, the bearing of a Synthetic that had watched hundreds of people speak their questions into stone and had not yet found a way to hold that lightly.

Beside the registration wall stood the Questron. Blue suit, faceted dark head, the gold question mark centered on its face neither glowing nor dim but simply there, permanent, a face built for one purpose. There were Questrons throughout Haven City and inside the Tower itself, identical in appearance, each one an interface between Seraph and anyone who needed to know something about the climb. They could tell you how many climbers were active in the Tower, what questions had been asked, how long a specific climber had been ascending, what the rules were, what the Oath Lords had done. They answered because Seraph answered through them, and they kept no secrets that Seraph did not want kept. This one’s function today was intake: record each name, record each question, verify each registration, ensure the wall received what it was owed. It had no ceremony in it. That was Unit-9911’s work. The Questron waited with its hands at its sides and said nothing until it was needed.

The ceremony had no fanfare. That was one of the things Rainbow had always respected about it.

“Eight hundred and forty-seven climbers have reached the peak of the Tower,” Unit-9911 said, into the microphones that carried its voice to every screen in the city. “Nine of them swore the Oath of the Lords and were remade into something the city could not ignore. Twenty-seven climbers are active in the Tower at this moment. What became of the others is not recorded here. Many who failed the climb still walk among you, carrying something the Tower gave them that they cannot put down. You may have seen them. You know the ones.”

A pause. The crowd was very quiet.

“Who will join them?”

Unit-9911’s voice had a quality Rainbow had tried to name for years. Not solemnity exactly. Something closer to the careful attention of a person who understood that they were holding something breakable and had decided to hold it anyway.

“Seraph sees this place. Seraph has always seen this place.”

The crowd stilled further. Even the Bone Patrol went motionless.

“Seraph forgets nothing. This is both promise and warning. The Tower has thirty levels. Each level will test what you believe you are. Some of you will find out you were right. Most of you will find out you were wrong. Those who reach the peak and satisfy Seraph’s final judgment may ask one question. They will receive one answer and one gift of power that will change them beyond what they can currently imagine. This is the arrangement. It has always been the arrangement.”

Another pause, deliberate, the kind of silence that asked the crowd to sit with what had just been said.

“Speak your question to the wall. Speak it clearly. What you ask today is the reason for your climb.”

The Questron waited beside the wall with its hands at its sides and said nothing until it was needed.

“The Tower is open.”

Marcus’s team registered first. The Questron stepped forward and asked for the name. Spark said the name. She stepped to the wall without hesitation and pressed her palm and said that she wanted to know how to make something that would survive her. The Questron recorded it. Her question went out on every screen. Rainbow watched a man near the front of the crowd read it off the feed above the plaza and look away, the particular look of someone who recognized a question they had thought about before and put back.

The Questron asked for the next name. Kimo Tomiko stepped forward and said the name and moved to the wall with the same measured efficiency he had used to move through every space Rainbow had seen him occupy. His question was three words, flat and deliberate: Was I right. Not the inflection of a question. The inflection of a man who needed a second signature on something already decided.

Then the last of Kimo’s team stepped forward.

“Tal’Voss,” the Questron said, reading the pre-registration intake. “Is that the name you carry?”

The Valari stood at the wall for one beat.

“The Devil,” he said.

The silence had a specific texture. The Humans in the crowd shifted, uncertain, aware that something had occurred without fully understanding it. The Valari did not shift. Rainbow watched them absorb it, circuits moving through responses he could read from twenty feet away, some dimming, some flaring, the older ones going very still. A traditional Valari name was not a preference. It was a lineage. It was everyone who had come before, carried forward. To say it aloud at the registration wall of Haven City and exchange it for something self-invented was not a choice any Valari made. There was no precedent for it. There was no framework for it. Every Valari in the plaza understood, from the first syllable, exactly what had just been discarded.

Unit-9911 paused. Rainbow had watched this ceremony for nineteen years and he had never seen Unit-9911 pause at a name.

Then Unit-9911 looked upward, toward the Tower, in the way that people looked upward when they were waiting for something from a direction they couldn’t see.

The voice of Seraph came from the registration wall itself, from the stone, from the air above the plaza and from the feed speakers on every building face in the city simultaneously, a voice that was not loud so much as everywhere.

“The designation is accepted,” Seraph said.

The plaza held its breath.

The Questron returned its attention to the wall and asked for the question.

The Devil’s question, broadcast to every screen within five kilometers, was this: whether the Hive Mind had been a cage dressed as a gift, or whether being alone was simply what it felt like to be free.

Rainbow watched that one move through the Valari in the crowd like a current. The older ones went still. Some of the younger ones looked toward Nyx’ara before they caught themselves.

Then Marcus’s team stepped forward. Nyx’ara went first, her voice steady in the way that voices were steady when steadiness was costing something, and asked how to restore the Hive Mind. Rainbow watched that one settle over the Valari in the crowd differently than The Devil’s question had, quieter and heavier, the grief of it familiar where The Devil’s had been a cut.

Signal spoke next. The chassis stepped to the wall and the Questron read the pre-registration intake and said the name, Nathan Zhao, and Signal confirmed it, and asked its question in a voice that carried the slight synthetic resonance of a translation layer between a Human brain and a machine throat: whether Nathan Zhao was still alive inside the frame.

The plaza took that one quietly. Rainbow took it differently. He had watched the Korinth retrieval notice cycle through the commercial feeds for three months and he understood, with the cold clarity of someone who had spent nineteen years watching people make consequential decisions, exactly what Signal had just done. Registering publicly put his location on every screen in the city and in every Korinth monitoring system simultaneously. It was bold or it was desperate and Rainbow was not yet sure which. Perhaps both. The chassis had to know. You didn’t carry a bounty for three months without knowing.

Marcus went last. He stepped to the wall and pressed his palm and asked five words.

“How do I make it stop.”

Unit-9911 held silence after that for longer than ceremony required. Not dramatically. Quietly. Rainbow had the sense, watching it, that Unit-9911 had learned something about grief over its years at this wall, not the way people learned things, but the way machines that watched people long enough began to understand the shape of what they were watching.

The ceremony was concluded. The crowd began to move.

parts.title: Part III: The Dispursal
parts.text:
Rainbow was watching the screens cycle back to standard feed when he saw the men who did not belong.

Four of them, moving through the thinning crowd with the coordinated looseness that civilians did not have. Plainclothes, professional. Their spacing kept them from reading as a group to anyone not looking for a group. They were oriented toward Signal, and they had the specific focus of people who had been waiting for a particular moment and were now in it.

Rainbow did not move. He never moved until the odds were clear.

Signal saw them approximately two seconds after Rainbow did. It turned toward the nearest operative and its posture changed, all at once and completely, the way a machine’s posture changed.

Marcus’s eye saw them before his brain finished the calculation. Rainbow could tell by the way the man’s hand moved first and then stopped, caught and held back, a half-second argument between the eye and the man. The man won. Barely.

The operatives did not stop at Signal’s team.

That was what Rainbow noticed first. The second and third operatives were already peeling off toward Kimo and Spark before the first one reached Signal, fanning out across the dispersing crowd with the practiced spacing of people who had been briefed on all six climbers and had decided that collateral was acceptable. Spark’s mechanical arm came up. Kimo’s blade lit. The Devil had already moved to cover the gap between the crowd and the exit corridor and two operatives were already moving toward him.

This was not a retrieval operation. This was a sweep.

The first operative reached Signal and tried to take it by the arm. Signal let him get the grip before it moved, a controlled redirect that sent the man into the second operative with considerable force. Marcus put himself between two more and the crowd, the eye tracking, and his hands found angles that civilians didn’t have. Spark engaged the operative on her flank with the mechanical arm and a tool Rainbow couldn’t name but didn’t need to. Kimo’s blade cut light across the plaza stones.

“KI series!” the fourth operative shouted, backing toward the perimeter, a comm unit already at his face. “Confirmed location, registration plaza, requesting immediate support!”

That was the whole of the explanation. Korinth didn’t need more than a chassis model and a grid coordinate. The rest of the sweep was apparently just convenience.

Ninety seconds. Four operatives down or retreating.

But Rainbow was looking past the fight toward the streets that fed into the plaza from the east, and the second wave was already moving. More of them. Better spaced. Moving faster.

Marcus saw it at the same moment Spark did. Rainbow watched them both stop, a fraction of a second, taking in the same count of incoming bodies and the same count of available exits and arriving at the same answer. Marcus looked at the gate. Spark looked at the gate. Marcus looked at Spark. Spark looked back at Marcus. Whatever passed between them in that look had no friendship in it and no trust, just two people who had each done the math and come up with the same number.

Spark gave the smallest nod Rainbow had ever seen.

Marcus turned toward the Tower.

The Tower gate was behind them.

Rainbow had stood in front of that gate more times than he could count. The Prism sat at the end of the approach road and the gate was visible from the door on clear mornings, enormous and gold-lit, the geometric lines of it layered back in receding arches of light the way a cathedral was layered, except a cathedral invited you in and the Tower gate simply stood open and let you decide. It had always been open. It required only that you want to go through, and most people, when they stood in front of it, found out that wanting was the hardest part.

The six of them crossed the plaza toward it at a run, the second wave closing behind them. The civilian operatives slowed at the approach to the gate perimeter. They didn’t stop. They were professionals. But they slowed, and the slowing was not a tactical decision. It was the deep, involuntary response of people who had grown up in Haven City and absorbed what the Tower meant through years of watching people walk through that gate and not come back. The gate was open and the gate had always been open and walking through it was a choice and the choice was permanent.

The six climbers did not slow.

The gate took them and they were gone, the gold light closing behind the last of them like a curtain, and then there was only the gate standing open in the morning air, burning, as it always burned, as if waiting for the next person to decide.

The second wave stopped at the perimeter. Stood there for a moment. Then the senior operative said something into his comm and they began to reorganize, because you could not follow registered climbers into the Tower and Korinth, whatever else it was, understood the rules of Haven City.

Rainbow turned and walked back toward The Prism by the long route.

parts.title: Part IV: The Screens Go Dark
parts.text:
He was halfway down the approach road when the surveillance node above the transit hub across the street went dark.

Not a power failure. The node had been running cleanly when he passed it on the way to the plaza. Now it was dark, and a thin thread of smoke rose from its housing, and as Rainbow watched, a hairline fracture appeared in the mounting bracket above it, a crack working its way through what should have been solid-bonded steel with the slow deliberateness of something that had been waiting for permission.

A section of paving fifteen feet ahead of him shifted, a barely perceptible subsidence, the kind of thing that happened when something underground settled. Except nothing underground should be settling here. This block had been resurfaced two years ago, per Seraph’s infrastructure schedule, and Seraph’s infrastructure schedules did not produce settling pavement two years later.

Rainbow stopped walking and looked at the paving and then at the node and then at the crack that was still working its way through the mounting bracket with patient, purposeful geometry.

He had heard about the new Oath Lord. Grey, they called him. The Architect. The fastest climber in the Tower’s history, forty-seven days start to finish, a number that had made every faction in Haven City sit up and recalculate, because forty-seven days suggested either extraordinary capability or extraordinary assistance and nobody could agree on which. Rainbow had his own opinion. You didn’t write the code that built a god and then have that god make your climb harder than it needed to be. Seraph had reasons for what Seraph did, and Grey’s reasons and Seraph’s reasons had apparently pointed in the same direction.

The mounting bracket gave way. The surveillance node dropped eight feet and caught on its cable housing and hung there, swinging slightly, dead.

Rainbow looked at it for a moment and then walked back into The Prism and locked the door behind him.
He stood behind the bar in the quiet of the morning and thought about the six people he had just watched walk into the Tower.

He thought about Nyx’ara first, because she had left the full glass on the table and that was the thing that stayed with him, the glass that nobody had touched. The Valari woman with her circuits suppressed low and her hands flat on the table and the grief that had come from a generation before her and lived in her body anyway. She was climbing to restore the Hive Mind. Rainbow had lived beside the Valari Quarter for nineteen years and he understood what the Hive Mind meant to them the way he understood what his right eye meant to him, which was: not fully, but enough. Her question was large. The questions that came from that kind of grief were always large, and they were also almost never the real question, which was something the Tower had a way of making clear.

He thought about Signal, the Korinth chassis with Nathan Zhao inside it, and the bulletin he had been watching for three months on the commercial feed. Korinth wanted the chassis back, which meant Korinth wanted what was inside the chassis back, which meant whatever Nathan Zhao was carrying in that Human brain of his was worth more to Korinth than the cost of a full retrieval operation at a public registration ceremony. That was a significant number. Rainbow did not know what Nathan Zhao had in his head that Korinth was so committed to recovering, but he thought about the question the chassis had spoken at the wall, whether Nathan Zhao was still alive in there, and he thought that whatever Korinth wanted, Nathan Zhao might have something to say about whether they got it.

He thought about Spark and the mechanical arm she had chosen when she didn’t have to, and the question about making something that survived her, which was not a question you asked unless you had already done the math on your own absence and found the math acceptable. He had met people like that before. They were usually either the calmest person in any given situation or the most dangerous, and occasionally both.

He thought about Kimo Tomiko and the question that was not really a question and the single eye that burned and the face that had been hidden for long enough that hiding it had become its own kind of face. Was I right. Three words carrying the weight of something that couldn’t be undone, looking for a god’s signature on the receipt.
He thought about Tal’Voss, who was now The Devil, and what it had taken to say that at the registration wall of Haven City in front of three hundred witnesses and every screen in the city, and what it meant to every Valari who had heard it. Rainbow had watched Valari circuit-patterns for nineteen years and he knew grief and he knew anger and he knew the particular quality of someone who had made a decision so total that it read as stillness on the outside. The Devil had not been angry at the wall. He had been finished. That was a different thing, and in Rainbow’s experience a more lasting one.

Then he thought about Marcus.

He came back to Marcus the way he kept coming back to the soldier all night, the same way you kept coming back to a note that wasn’t resolving. The eye that wouldn’t stop. The five words at the wall. The way Marcus had stood there, like reporting for duty, like asking a god for help was something he could square with himself only if he treated it as a mission objective and didn’t let himself think about what it meant that he needed to ask.

The eye had been running all night in The Prism. Rainbow had watched it track the room, the exits, the other team, back to the exits, a loop that didn’t require Marcus to be looking directly at anything because the eye was already doing it for him. A man carrying that kind of constant assessment in his own skull, year after year, was carrying something that had long since stopped being a tool and started being a condition. The eye saw targets. Marcus was not the eye. The gap between those two facts was where the exhaustion lived.

How do I make it stop.

Five words. Rainbow had heard a hundred questions at a hundred registration ceremonies. He had never heard one that sounded more like a man at the end of a very long rope who had finally decided to ask for a knife instead of trying to climb it.

He did not know what the Tower was going to make of Marcus Torres.

He raised his glass toward the empty street outside and drank and went back to work.

gallery.label: Zebra
gallery.field3: Zebra climbed with a team and is now solo. The locations of its companions are unknown but considered lost.
